plainMeaning: It pictures the heavenly women near the Jina — their bodies gently bowed by full breasts, hips wrapped in loose gold-and-jewel girdles, jingling with anklets and bangles, lovely and captivating to behold.
recitation:
pīṇa nirantara thaṇabhara viṇamia gāyalaāhiṁ |
maṇi kaṁcaṇa pasiḍhila mahala sohia soṇitaḍāhiṁ |
vara khiṁkhiṇi neura satilaya valaya vibhūsaṇiāhiṁ,
raïkara caura maṇohara suṁdara daṁsaṇiāhiṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit pīna (‘swollen, full’); a stock śṛṅgāra epithet for the bosom.

wordByWord.gloss: close-set, with no gap between, unbroken
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit nis- (‘without’) + antara (‘interval’) = ‘gapless’; here the breasts pressed close together.
thaṇa (Skt. stana, ‘breast’) + bhara (Skt. bhara, ‘load, weight’).
wordByWord.notes: The instrumental cause of the body’s bending.

wordByWord.etymology: Past participle from Skt. vi-√nam ‘to bend’, vinamita.
wordByWord.notes: Their gait is slightly stooped — a poetic mark of grace.

wordByWord.etymology: gāya (Skt. gātra, ‘body, limb’) + affectionate-diminutive -la + fem. instr. pl. -āhiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: One of the four parallel ‘-āhiṁ’ instrumentals; all describe the devasundarīs governed from the surrounding verses.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. maṇi.
wordByWord.notes: maṇi-kaṁcaṇa = jewel-and-gold, the material of the girdle.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. kāñcana (‘gold’).
Skt. praśithila (pra- + śithila, ‘loose’); the girdle riding low and loose on the hip.
Best read as a contracted Apabhraṁśa form of mekhalā (‘waist-girdle’) → mehalā → mahala.
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; the maṇi-kaṁcaṇa-pasiḍhila context on the hip strongly favours ‘girdle’. See notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Past participle from Skt. √śubh ‘to shine’, śobhita.
soṇi (Skt. śroṇi, ‘hip, buttock’) + taḍa (Skt. taṭa, ‘bank, slope’) + fem. instr. pl. -āhiṁ; ‘the sloping banks of the hips’.
wordByWord.notes: The taṭa (‘riverbank’) metaphor for the swelling hip is classical kāvya.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vara (‘best, choice’).
Skt. kiṅkiṇī (‘small bell’); the form is onomatopoeic for the jingling sound.
Skt. nūpura (‘anklet’) → Prakrit ṇeura/neura.
sa- (‘with’) + tilaya (Skt. tilaka, ‘forehead-mark, ornamental spot’).
wordByWord.notes: Read here as ‘adorned with tilaka’; some recensions group it among the body-ornaments.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. valaya (‘circlet, bangle’).
Skt. vibhūṣaṇa (‘adornment’) + -iā + fem. instr. pl. -āhiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Sums up the preceding list of jewellery.

wordByWord.etymology: rai (Skt. rati, ‘delight, love’) + kara (‘making, causing’) = Skt. ratikara.
Skt. catura (‘skilful, charming’).
Skt. manohara (manas, ‘mind/heart’ + hara, ‘stealing’).
Skt. sundara.

wordByWord.gloss: (by/with them who are) lovely to behold, in their appearance
wordByWord.etymology: daṁsaṇa (Skt. darśana, ‘sight, appearance’) + -iā + fem. instr. pl. -āhiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The closing instrumental rounds off the fourfold portrait.

literalTranslation: With slender bodies bowed by the close-set weight of full breasts; with hip-slopes made resplendent by loose girdles of jewel and gold; bedecked with fine tinkling bells, anklets, tilaka-marks and bangles; delight-giving, graceful, heart-captivating, beautiful to behold —
idiomaticTranslation: Their slender forms bend gently beneath the swell of full, close-set breasts; their hips ride a loose girdle of gem and gold; fine anklets and bangles ring with tiny bells, and tilaka adorns them — delighting the eye, graceful and captivating, a vision lovely to look upon.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza extends the moving portrait of the heavenly women begun just before, where they were swan-gaited and lotus-eyed. Here the poet lingers on form and finery: the four lines each close on a parallel feminine instrumental in ‘-āhiṁ’ (gāyalaāhiṁ, soṇitaḍāhiṁ, vibhūsaṇiāhiṁ, daṁsaṇiāhiṁ), a deliberate metrical chime that lets the description cascade in a single breathless period. The imagery is frank kāvya śṛṅgāra — pīṇa-thaṇabhara (full breasts), soṇi-taḍa (hips likened to ‘riverbanks’), the low-slung jewelled mahala (girdle) — yet it is grammatically incomplete on its own: every ‘-āhiṁ’ form hangs, awaiting its verb. That verb arrives only in the following verse, where these same women are ‘pāyavaṁdiāhiṁ’ — bowing at the Jina’s feet. The suspended syntax is the theology: all this catalogued allure is left dangling until it resolves in an act of worship. Sound reinforces sense — khiṁkhiṇi (tinkling bells) and neura (anklets) fill the line with the music of their movement, the very jingle of devotion approaching. The closing quartet — raïkara, caura, maṇohara, suṁdara — caps beauty with grace and charm, completing the figure the next verse will bend to the ground.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse stages a characteristic Jain inversion of the erotic. Worldly poetry praises the beautiful woman as an object of desire; here that whole vocabulary of allure is mustered only to be surrendered at the feet of the vītarāga (the passion-free Jina). The Tīrthaṅkara, utterly dispassionate, neither sees nor is moved by this splendour — and that is precisely the point: the most captivating beauty in the cosmos voluntarily lays itself before one untouched by it. The devasundarīs model bhakti for the human reciter — even celestial fortune and form find their fulfilment not in display but in homage.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter is invited to read every ornament as a metaphor for offering: just as the heavenly women carry their finest adornments only to bow them down, one brings one’s own best — attention, voice, the body’s stillness in kāyotsarga — and sets it at the Jina’s feet. Beauty and possession are not condemned but redirected; what is loveliest is what is given up in devotion.

crossReferences.text: Following verse (smaran-2-ajitshanti/28): ‘devasundarīhiṁ pāyavaṁdiāhiṁ…’ — the same women bow at the Jina’s ‘invincible feet’ (suvikkamā kamā).
Bhaktāmara Stotra vv. on the assembly’s devotion — comparable subordination of cosmic grandeur to the Jina.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth — Digital Dictionaries of South Asia
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses and Sanskrit equivalents for thaṇa (stana), soṇi (śroṇi), taḍa (taṭa), ṇeura (nūpura), valaya, vibhūsaṇa, kiṅkiṇī.

sources.title: Prakrit Dictionary by ISJS — English translation of Pāia-Sadda-Mahaṇṇavo
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking romanized Prakrit roots and Sanskrit equivalents (pīṇa, niraṁtara, vinamia, daṁsaṇa).

sources.title: Jain stotra anthologies / Ajitaśānti Stava recensions (jainQQ digital library)
sources.consultedFor: Placement of the devasundarī tableau and resolution of the suspended ‘-āhiṁ’ instrumentals in the following verse (pāyavaṁdiāhiṁ).

notes: Every token is glossed (100% coverage). The four line-final ‘-āhiṁ’ words are feminine instrumental/locative plurals that remain syntactically dependent on the surrounding verses (the devasundarīs of v.26 and the ‘bowing at the feet’ verb of v.28); the stanza is grammatically a continuation, not a self-standing sentence. ‘મહલ / mahala’ (line 2) is the least certain item: read here as a contracted Apabhraṁśa mekhalā (‘girdle’) on the strength of the maṇi-kaṁcaṇa-pasiḍhila-soṇi context; some readers take it adjectivally (‘great/ample’). ‘સતિલય / satilaya’ is read as sa-tilaya (‘with tilaka’); a minority parsing folds it into the ornament-list. Orthography (doubled ‘આઆ’, anusvāra variants) is preserved verbatim from the book, not normalized.
